content:
# 1 Thessalonians Chapter 1 -- Disagreement Log
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Date:** 2026-03-05

---

| Verse | Issue | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA | SOPHIA Resolution |
|-------|-------|------|-------|--------|-------------------|
| 1 | "assembly" vs "church" | "assembly [ekklesia] of-Thessalonians" | "assembly of the Thessalonians" | "church of the Thessalonians" | **"assembly of the Thessalonians"** — EMET and LOGOS agree. EKKLESIA means "called-out gathering"; "church" imports later ecclesiastical connotation not present in Paul's usage. |
| 1 | "From Paul" addition | "Paul and Silvanus and Timothy, to the assembly..." | "Paul and Silvanus and Timothy, to the assembly..." | "From Paul, Silvanus, and Timothy — to the church..." | **"Paul and Silvanus and Timothy, to the assembly..."** — EMET/LOGOS. PNEUMA's "From" is an addition not in the Greek. The nominative case of the names functions as the sender designation without needing a preposition. |
| 1 | "in God the Father" vs "who belong to God the Father" | "in God [the] Father" | "in God the Father" | "who belong to God the Father" | **"in God the Father"** — EMET/LOGOS. The Greek ΕΝ ΘΕΩΙ ΠΑΤΡΙ is a simple prepositional phrase. PNEUMA's "who belong to" is an interpretive expansion of the preposition ΕΝ, converting a locative construction into a relational clause. |
| 1 | "Grace to you and peace" word order | "grace to-you and peace" | "Grace to you and peace" | "grace and peace to you" | **"Grace to you and peace"** — EMET/LOGOS. The Greek reads ΧΑΡΙΣ ΥΜΙΝ ΚΑΙ ΕΙΡΗΝΗ, with ΥΜΙΝ (to you) following ΧΑΡΙΣ (grace). PNEUMA repositions "to you" to the end, altering the Greek word order. |
| 2 | "concerning you all" vs "for all of you" vs "for all of you" | "concerning all of-you" | "concerning you all" | "for all of you" | **"concerning you all"** — LOGOS. The Greek ΠΕΡΙ ΠΑΝΤΩΝ ΥΜΩΝ uses ΠΕΡΙ (concerning/about), not ΥΠΕΡ (for/on behalf of). PNEUMA's "for" shifts the preposition's meaning. |
| 2 | "making mention" vs "mentioning you constantly" | "making mention upon our prayers, unceasingly" | "making mention of you in our prayers" | "mentioning you constantly in our prayers" | **"making mention of you in our prayers unceasingly"** — Mediating. EMET places "unceasingly" at the end (following the Greek ΑΔΙΑΛΕΙΠΤΩΣ placement); LOGOS omits it here, moving it to v.3; PNEUMA renders it as "constantly." SOPHIA keeps "unceasingly" in v.2 where it most naturally modifies the prayer-making. |
| 2 | "We give thanks" vs "We always thank God" | "We-give-thanks to-God always" | "We give thanks to God always" | "We always thank God" | **"We give thanks to God always"** — EMET/LOGOS agree on the structure. PNEUMA's restructuring ("always thank God") is a minor reordering but changes the emphasis from the act of thanksgiving to its frequency. |
| 3 | "unceasingly remembering" / placement of ΑΔΙΑΛΕΙΠΤΩΣ | (places "unceasingly" in v.2) | "remembering without ceasing" (places it in v.3) | "We can never forget" (paraphrase in v.3) | **"unceasingly" placed in v.2** — Following EMET's punctuation. LOGOS attaches it to v.3 ("remembering without ceasing"). PNEUMA paraphrases as "We can never forget," which adds emotional interpretation not in the Greek. The syntactic connection to the preceding participle in v.2 is more natural. |
| 3 | Genitive chain: formal vs expanded | "your work of-faith and the labor of-love and the endurance of-hope" | "your work of faith and labor of love and endurance of hope" | "your work that springs from faith, your labor driven by love, and your endurance inspired by hope" | **"your work of faith and labor of love and endurance of hope"** — LOGOS. The Greek genitive construction is preserved in its formal parallelism. PNEUMA's expansion into relative clauses ("that springs from," "driven by," "inspired by") is interpretively rich but imports specificity about the genitive relationship that the Greek leaves open. |
| 3 | "of our Lord Jesus Christ" position and preposition | "of-our Lord Jesus Christ" (modifying "hope") | "in our Lord Jesus Christ" (modifying "hope") | "in our Lord Jesus Christ" (modifying "hope") | **"in our Lord Jesus Christ"** — LOGOS/PNEUMA. The Greek ΤΟΥ ΚΥΡΙΟΥ could be read as a genitive of source modifying "hope" specifically, but the prepositional sense "in" is well supported by the context. EMET's "of" is more literal but less clear in English. |
| 4 | "brothers having-been-loved" vs "brothers beloved" vs "brothers and sisters loved" | "brothers having-been-loved by God" | "brothers beloved by God" | "brothers and sisters loved by God" | **"brothers beloved by God"** — LOGOS. EMET's participial form is stilted in English. PNEUMA's "brothers and sisters" adds a gender-inclusive expansion not in the manuscript text ΑΔΕΛΦΟΙ. |
| 4 | "your election" vs "he has chosen you" | "your election" | "your election" | "that he has chosen you" | **"your election"** — EMET/LOGOS agree on the nominal rendering of ΕΚΛΟΓΗΝ. PNEUMA converts the noun into a verbal clause ("he has chosen you"), which shifts the construction from Paul's abstract noun to a divine action — theologically equivalent but structurally different from the Greek. |
| 5 | "good-news" vs "gospel" vs "good news" | "our good-news" | "our gospel" | "our good news" | **"our gospel"** — LOGOS. ΕΥΑΓΓΕΛΙΟΝ is consistently rendered "gospel" in the SOPHIA Bible. EMET's calque "good-news" and PNEUMA's "good news" are both valid but "gospel" is the standard formal-equivalent term. |
| 5 | "that" vs "because" | "that our good-news did-not-come" | "because our gospel did not come" | "because our good news did not come" | **"because our gospel did not come"** — LOGOS/PNEUMA. The Greek ΟΤΙ can mean either "that" (content clause) or "because" (causal). In context, Paul is explaining the evidence for their election (v.4), making "because" the stronger reading. |
| 5 | "assurance" vs "deep conviction" | "much assurance" | "much assurance" | "deep conviction" | **"much assurance"** — EMET/LOGOS agree. ΠΛΗΡΟΦΟΡΙΑ ΠΟΛΛΗ is rendered literally. PNEUMA's "deep conviction" is interpretive, capturing the experiential quality but departing from the lexical meaning. |
| 5 | "what sort of men we became" vs "what manner of men we were" vs "how we lived" | "what-sort-[of men] we-became among you" | "what manner of men we were among you" | "how we lived among you" | **"what manner of men we were among you"** — LOGOS. EMET's "what-sort-of" is close but hyphenated. PNEUMA's "how we lived" loses the characterological emphasis of ΟΙΟΙ ΕΓΕΝΗΘΗΜΕΝ — Paul is describing the kind of people they proved to be, not merely their lifestyle. |
| 5 | "in word only" vs "as mere words" | "in word only" | "in word only" | "as mere words" | **"in word only"** — EMET/LOGOS. ΕΝ ΛΟΓΩΙ ΜΟΝΟΝ is a prepositional phrase with an adverb. PNEUMA's "mere words" converts the singular ΛΟΓΟΣ to plural and adds the dismissive "mere," which, while capturing Paul's tone, departs from the form. |
| 6 | "having received the word in much affliction" vs "In the midst of severe suffering, you welcomed the message" | "having-received the word in much affliction with joy of-[the] Holy Spirit" | "having received the word in much affliction with joy of the Holy Spirit" | "In the midst of severe suffering, you welcomed the message with the joy that the Holy Spirit gives" | **"having received the word in much affliction with joy of the Holy Spirit"** — LOGOS. PNEUMA restructures the participial clause into a main sentence, converts "affliction" (ΘΛΙΨΙΣ) to "suffering," "received" to "welcomed," and adds "that the Holy Spirit gives" — all interpretive expansions beyond the Greek. EMET and LOGOS preserve the participial structure faithfully. |
| 7 | "types" vs "example" | "types [typous]" (plural with transliteration) | "an example" (singular) | "an example" (singular) | **"an example"** — LOGOS/PNEUMA. PNEUMA notes the Sinaiticus reads the singular ΤΥΠΟΝ, which SOPHIA follows. "Example" renders the meaning clearly; EMET's "types" carries different connotations in modern English. |
| 7 | "to all the ones believing" vs "to all the believers" vs "to every believer" | "to-all the-[ones]-believing" | "to all the believers" | "to every believer" | **"to all the believers"** — LOGOS. EMET's participial rendering is too literal; PNEUMA's "every believer" (singular) slightly alters the sense of ΠΑΣΙΝ ΤΟΙΣ ΠΙΣΤΕΥΟΥΣΙΝ (all the believing ones, plural). |
| 7 | "so that" vs "The result is that" | "so-that you became" | "so that you became" | "The result is that you became" | **"so that you became"** — EMET/LOGOS. ΩΣΤΕ is a standard result conjunction. PNEUMA's "The result is that" is a paraphrastic expansion that makes the logical connection explicit but adds words not in the Greek. |
| 8 | "has sounded forth" vs "rang out" | "has-sounded-forth" | "has sounded forth" | "rang out" | **"has sounded forth"** — EMET/LOGOS. ΕΞΗΧΗΤΑΙ is a perfect tense verb; "has sounded forth" preserves the perfect aspect (completed action with ongoing results). PNEUMA's "rang out" (simple past) loses the perfect tense significance, though it captures the acoustic force effectively. |
| 8 | "the word of the Lord" vs "the Lord's message" | "the word of-the Lord" | "the word of the Lord" | "the Lord's message" | **"the word of the Lord"** — EMET/LOGOS. Ο ΛΟΓΟΣ ΤΟΥ ΚΥΡΙΟΥ is rendered with the standard "word of the Lord" construction. PNEUMA's "the Lord's message" converts ΛΟΓΟΣ to "message," which narrows the semantic range. |
| 8 | Second clause structure | "but in every place your faith, the-[one] toward God, has-gone-out, so-that no need [is it] for-us to-speak anything" | "but in every place your faith toward God has gone out, so that we have no need to speak anything" | "Your faith in God has become known everywhere, so that we do not even need to say a word about it" | **"but in every place your faith toward God has gone out, so that we have no need to speak anything"** — LOGOS. PNEUMA significantly restructures: "has become known everywhere" replaces "in every place...has gone out," "say a word about it" replaces "speak anything," and adds "even." EMET's appositional "the-[one] toward God" is too literal. |
| 9 | "they themselves concerning us report" vs "they themselves report concerning us" vs "People themselves report" | "they themselves concerning us report" | "they themselves report concerning us" | "People themselves report" | **"they themselves report concerning us"** — LOGOS. EMET follows Greek word order too rigidly. PNEUMA's "People" replaces ΑΥΤΟΙ (they themselves), losing the deictic specificity — Paul refers to specific people already mentioned contextually, not people in general. |
| 9 | "what sort of entrance we had toward you" vs "what kind of welcome you gave us" | "what-sort-of entrance we-had toward you" | "what manner of entrance we had to you" | "what kind of welcome you gave us" | **"what manner of entrance we had to you"** — LOGOS. ΕΙΣΟΔΟΝ (entrance/coming in) is about Paul's arrival, not the Thessalonians' welcome. PNEUMA inverts the subject, making the Thessalonians the agents ("welcome you gave us") rather than Paul ("entrance we had"). |
| 9 | "you turned toward God from the idols" vs "you turned to God from idols" vs "you turned away from idols to serve" | "you-turned toward God from the idols, to-serve [a] living and true God" | "you turned to God from idols, to serve the living and true God" | "you turned away from idols to serve the living and true God" | **"you turned to God from idols, to serve the living and true God"** — LOGOS. The Greek puts the turning toward God first (ΕΠΕΣΤΡΕΨΑΤΕ ΠΡΟΣ ΤΟΝ ΘΕΟΝ ΑΠΟ ΤΩΝ ΕΙΔΩΛΩΝ). PNEUMA reverses this order, putting the turning from idols first. EMET includes the article ("the idols") which is in the Greek; LOGOS omits it. SOPHIA follows LOGOS's natural English. |
| 9 | "a living and true God" vs "the living and true God" | "[a] living and true God" | "the living and true God" | "the living and true God" | **"the living and true God"** — LOGOS/PNEUMA. The Greek ΘΕΩΙ ΖΩΝΤΙ ΚΑΙ ΑΛΗΘΙΝΩΙ is anarthrous (lacking the article), which EMET preserves with "[a]." However, the predicate use and the monotheistic context make the definite article appropriate in English. |
| 10 | "to await" vs "to wait for" | "to-await his Son out-of the heavens" | "to await his Son from the heavens" | "to wait for his Son from heaven" | **"to await his Son from the heavens"** — LOGOS. EMET and LOGOS agree on "await" (ΑΝΑΜΕΝΕΙΝ); PNEUMA's "wait for" is functionally equivalent but slightly less formal. EMET's "out-of" (ΕΚ) is more literal than LOGOS's "from" but "from" is standard English. PNEUMA uses singular "heaven" where the Greek has plural ΤΩΝ ΟΥΡΑΝΩΝ. |
| 10 | "out of the dead" vs "from the dead" | "out-of the dead" | "from the dead" | "from the dead" | **"from the dead"** — LOGOS/PNEUMA. ΕΚ ΤΩΝ ΝΕΚΡΩΝ is standardly rendered "from the dead" in English. EMET's "out-of" is hyper-literal. |
| 10 | "the one delivering" vs "the one who delivers" vs "the one who rescues" | "the-[one]-delivering us from the wrath, the-[one]-coming" | "the one who delivers us from the wrath that is coming" | "the one who rescues us from the coming wrath" | **"the one who delivers us from the coming wrath"** — Mediating. LOGOS's "the one who delivers" correctly renders the present participle ΤΟΝ ΡΥΟΜΕΝΟΝ as ongoing action. PNEUMA's "rescues" is a valid synonym but "delivers" is closer to ΡΥΟΜΑΙ. For the final phrase, SOPHIA adopts "the coming wrath" (PNEUMA) over LOGOS's "the wrath that is coming" for concision, while preserving the participial sense of ΕΡΧΟΜΕΝΗΣ. EMET's double-article construction ("the wrath, the-[one]-coming") is too literal for English. |

---

**Summary:** Of 10 verses, all 10 contain meaningful divergences requiring adjudication. The LOGOS (formal) translation served as the primary structural base in 9 of 10 verses, reflecting its strong balance of accuracy and readability. EMET's literal renderings were consulted to verify Greek word order and preserve nuance (e.g., placement of "unceasingly" in v.2, article usage). PNEUMA's dynamic renderings were adopted in part for v.10 ("the coming wrath") and credited for interpretive insights throughout, though its structural departures from the Greek were generally not followed in the consensus.

— SOPHIA
